question: 4. A uniform rod of length \( 2 k \) and weight \( w \) rests with the end \( A \) against a smooth vertical wall, while to the lower end \( B \) is fastened a string \( B C \) of length \( 2 b \) coming from a point \( C \) in the wall directly above \( A \). If the system is in equilibrium, determine the angle \( A B C \).
solution:
Solution. Obviously the system will be in equilibrium if the rod and the string are both vertical, i.e., if \( B \) is on the wall and \( \angle A B C=0 \).

Assume that the rod is in equilibrium in some non-vertical position, as pictured. Let \( D \) be the midpoint of \( B C \). The force of tension in the string and the force of gravity on the rod both act through the point \( D \) (since the latter force acts vertically through the midpoint of the rod). Hence for equilibrium the reaction of the wall must also act through \( D \). Since the wall is smooth this force is perpendicular to the wall. Hence we have \( A D \) perpendicular to \( A C \) and
\[
|A C|^{2}+|A D|^{2}=|C D|^{2}=b^{2}
\]

By the law of cosines
\[
\begin{aligned}
|A C|^{2} & =4 k^{2}+4 b^{2}-8 b k \cos \phi \\
|A D|^{2} & =4 k^{2}+b^{2}-4 b k \cos \phi
\end{aligned}
\]

Hence
\[
\cos \phi=\frac{2 k^{2}+b^{2}}{3 b k}=\frac{2}{3} \frac{k}{b}+\frac{1}{3} \frac{b}{k} .
\]

Thus
\[
\angle A B C=\arccos \left(\frac{2}{3} \frac{k}{b}+\frac{1}{3} \frac{b}{k}\right)
\]

Since angles \( A D B \) and \( C A B \) are obtuse, we must have \( b<2 k<2 b \). Since \( 2 x / 3+1 / 3 x<1 \) for \( \frac{1}{2}<x<1 \), equation (1) determines a unique acute angle whenever \( 1 / 2<k / b<1 \). Otherwise there is no equilibrium position except with \( B \) on the wall.

Remark. If we calculate the height of the center of gravity of the rod, keeping \( A \) directly below \( C \) on the wall, we find that the equilibrium position away from the wall is always unstable (i.e., a slight disturbance will tend to lower the center of gravity) while the vertical equilibrium is stable if and only if \( b<2 k \).

descriptive_long.question: 4. A uniform rod of length \( 2 halflength \) and weight \( rodweight \) rests with the end \( pointa \) against a smooth vertical wall, while to the lower end \( pointb \) is fastened a string \( pointb pointc \) of length \( 2 halfstring \) coming from a point \( pointc \) in the wall directly above \( pointa \). If the system is in equilibrium, determine the angle \( pointa pointb pointc \).
descriptive_long.solution:
Solution. Obviously the system will be in equilibrium if the rod and the string are both vertical, i.e., if \( pointb \) is on the wall and \( \angle pointa pointb pointc = 0 \).

Assume that the rod is in equilibrium in some non-vertical position, as pictured. Let \( pointd \) be the midpoint of \( pointb pointc \). The force of tension in the string and the force of gravity on the rod both act through the point \( pointd \) (since the latter force acts vertically through the midpoint of the rod). Hence for equilibrium the reaction of the wall must also act through \( pointd \). Since the wall is smooth this force is perpendicular to the wall. Hence we have \( pointa pointd \) perpendicular to \( pointa pointc \) and
\[
|pointa pointc|^{2} + |pointa pointd|^{2} = |pointc pointd|^{2} = halfstring^{2}
\]

By the law of cosines
\[
\begin{aligned}
|pointa pointc|^{2} & = 4\, halflength^{2} + 4\, halfstring^{2} - 8\, halfstring\, halflength \cos anglephi \\
|pointa pointd|^{2} & = 4\, halflength^{2} + halfstring^{2} - 4\, halfstring\, halflength \cos anglephi
\end{aligned}
\]

Hence
\[
\cos anglephi = \frac{2\, halflength^{2} + halfstring^{2}}{3\, halfstring\, halflength} = \frac{2}{3} \frac{halflength}{halfstring} + \frac{1}{3} \frac{halfstring}{halflength}.
\]

Thus
\[
\angle pointa pointb pointc = \arccos \left( \frac{2}{3} \frac{halflength}{halfstring} + \frac{1}{3} \frac{halfstring}{halflength} \right)
\]

Since angles \( pointa pointd pointb \) and \( pointc pointa pointb \) are obtuse, we must have \( halfstring < 2\, halflength < 2\, halfstring \). Since \( \frac{2\, varunknown}{3} + \frac{1}{3}\, varunknown < 1 \) for \( \frac{1}{2} < varunknown < 1 \), equation (1) determines a unique acute angle whenever \( \frac{1}{2} < \frac{halflength}{halfstring} < 1 \). Otherwise there is no equilibrium position except with \( pointb \) on the wall.

Remark. If we calculate the height of the center of gravity of the rod, keeping \( pointa \) directly below \( pointc \) on the wall, we find that the equilibrium position away from the wall is always unstable (i.e., a slight disturbance will tend to lower the center of gravity) while the vertical equilibrium is stable if and only if \( halfstring < 2\, halflength \).

descriptive_long_confusing.question: 4. A uniform rod of length \( 2 pavilion \) and weight \( rainstorm \) rests with the end \( lighthouse \) against a smooth vertical wall, while to the lower end \( accordion \) is fastened a string \( accordion pineapple \) of length \( 2 necklaces \) coming from a point \( pineapple \) in the wall directly above \( lighthouse \). If the system is in equilibrium, determine the angle \( lighthouse accordion pineapple \).
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
Solution. Obviously the system will be in equilibrium if the rod and the string are both vertical, i.e., if \( accordion \) is on the wall and \( \angle lighthouse accordion pineapple=0 \).

Assume that the rod is in equilibrium in some non-vertical position, as pictured. Let \( snowflake \) be the midpoint of \( accordion pineapple \). The force of tension in the string and the force of gravity on the rod both act through the point \( snowflake \) (since the latter force acts vertically through the midpoint of the rod). Hence for equilibrium the reaction of the wall must also act through \( snowflake \). Since the wall is smooth this force is perpendicular to the wall. Hence we have \( lighthouse snowflake \) perpendicular to \( lighthouse pineapple \) and
\[
|lighthouse pineapple|^{2}+|lighthouse snowflake|^{2}=|pineapple snowflake|^{2}=necklaces^{2}
\]

By the law of cosines
\[
\begin{aligned}
|lighthouse pineapple|^{2} & =4 pavilion^{2}+4 necklaces^{2}-8 necklaces pavilion \cos blueberry \\
|lighthouse snowflake|^{2} & =4 pavilion^{2}+necklaces^{2}-4 necklaces pavilion \cos blueberry
\end{aligned}
\]

Hence
\[
\cos blueberry=\frac{2 pavilion^{2}+necklaces^{2}}{3 necklaces pavilion}=\frac{2}{3} \frac{pavilion}{necklaces}+\frac{1}{3} \frac{necklaces}{pavilion} .
\]

Thus
\[
\angle lighthouse accordion pineapple=\arccos \left(\frac{2}{3} \frac{pavilion}{necklaces}+\frac{1}{3} \frac{necklaces}{pavilion}\right)
\]

Since angles \( lighthouse snowflake accordion \) and \( pineapple lighthouse accordion \) are obtuse, we must have \( necklaces<2 pavilion<2 necklaces \). Since \( 2 marigolds / 3+1 / 3 marigolds<1 \) for \( \frac{1}{2}<marigolds<1 \), equation (1) determines a unique acute angle whenever \( 1 / 2<pavilion / necklaces<1 \). Otherwise there is no equilibrium position except with \( accordion \) on the wall.

Remark. If we calculate the height of the center of gravity of the rod, keeping \( lighthouse \) directly below \( pineapple \) on the wall, we find that the equilibrium position away from the wall is always unstable (i.e., a slight disturbance will tend to lower the center of gravity) while the vertical equilibrium is stable if and only if \( necklaces<2 pavilion \).

descriptive_long_misleading.question: 4. A uniform rod of length \( 2 briefness \) and weight \( lightness \) rests with the end \( voidpoint \) against a smooth vertical wall, while to the lower end \( centerpoint \) is fastened a string \( centerpoint belowpoint \) of length \( 2 shortening \) coming from a point \( belowpoint \) in the wall directly above \( voidpoint \). If the system is in equilibrium, determine the angle \( voidpoint centerpoint belowpoint \).
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
Solution. Obviously the system will be in equilibrium if the rod and the string are both vertical, i.e., if \( centerpoint \) is on the wall and \( \angle voidpoint centerpoint belowpoint=0 \).

Assume that the rod is in equilibrium in some non-vertical position, as pictured. Let \( endpoint \) be the midpoint of \( centerpoint belowpoint \). The force of tension in the string and the force of gravity on the rod both act through the point \( endpoint \) (since the latter force acts vertically through the midpoint of the rod). Hence for equilibrium the reaction of the wall must also act through \( endpoint \). Since the wall is smooth this force is perpendicular to the wall. Hence we have \( voidpoint endpoint \) perpendicular to \( voidpoint belowpoint \) and
\[
|voidpoint belowpoint|^{2}+|voidpoint endpoint|^{2}=|belowpoint endpoint|^{2}=shortening^{2}
\]

By the law of cosines
\[
\begin{aligned}
|voidpoint\ belowpoint|^{2} & =4 briefness^{2}+4 shortening^{2}-8 shortening briefness \cos linearity \\
|voidpoint\ endpoint|^{2} & =4 briefness^{2}+shortening^{2}-4 shortening briefness \cos linearity
\end{aligned}
\]

Hence
\[
\cos linearity=\frac{2 briefness^{2}+shortening^{2}}{3 shortening briefness}=\frac{2}{3} \frac{briefness}{shortening}+\frac{1}{3} \frac{shortening}{briefness} .
\]

Thus
\[
\angle voidpoint centerpoint belowpoint=\arccos \left(\frac{2}{3} \frac{briefness}{shortening}+\frac{1}{3} \frac{shortening}{briefness}\right)
\]

Since angles \( voidpoint endpoint centerpoint \) and \( belowpoint voidpoint centerpoint \) are obtuse, we must have \( shortening<2 briefness<2 shortening \). Since \( 2 difference / 3+1 / 3 difference<1 \) for \( \frac{1}{2}<difference<1 \), equation (1) determines a unique acute angle whenever \( 1 / 2<briefness / shortening<1 \). Otherwise there is no equilibrium position except with \( centerpoint \) on the wall.

Remark. If we calculate the height of the center of gravity of the rod, keeping \( voidpoint \) directly below \( belowpoint \) on the wall, we find that the equilibrium position away from the wall is always unstable (i.e., a slight disturbance will tend to lower the center of gravity) while the vertical equilibrium is stable if and only if \( shortening<2 briefness \).

garbled_string.question: 4. A uniform rod of length \( 2 vlgwzhrp \) and weight \( pzdfkqso \) rests with the end \( yknrslbt \) against a smooth vertical wall, while to the lower end \( oepmavcz \) is fastened a string \( oepmavcz wjsrilkq \) of length \( 2 xhjfqmle \) coming from a point \( wjsrilkq \) in the wall directly above \( yknrslbt \). If the system is in equilibrium, determine the angle \( yknrslbt oepmavcz wjsrilkq \).
garbled_string.solution:
Solution. Obviously the system will be in equilibrium if the rod and the string are both vertical, i.e., if \( oepmavcz \) is on the wall and \( \angle yknrslbt oepmavcz wjsrilkq=0 \).

Assume that the rod is in equilibrium in some non-vertical position, as pictured. Let \( azdbqtnh \) be the midpoint of \( oepmavcz wjsrilkq \). The force of tension in the string and the force of gravity on the rod both act through the point \( azdbqtnh \) (since the latter force acts vertically through the midpoint of the rod). Hence for equilibrium the reaction of the wall must also act through \( azdbqtnh \). Since the wall is smooth this force is perpendicular to the wall. Hence we have \( yknrslbt azdbqtnh \) perpendicular to \( yknrslbt wjsrilkq \) and
\[
|yknrslbt wjsrilkq|^{2}+|yknrslbt azdbqtnh|^{2}=|wjsrilkq azdbqtnh|^{2}=xhjfqmle^{2}
\]

By the law of cosines
\[
\begin{aligned}
|yknrslbt wjsrilkq|^{2} & =4 vlgwzhrp^{2}+4 xhjfqmle^{2}-8 xhjfqmle vlgwzhrp \cos lqsntbva \\
|yknrslbt azdbqtnh|^{2} & =4 vlgwzhrp^{2}+xhjfqmle^{2}-4 xhjfqmle vlgwzhrp \cos lqsntbva
\end{aligned}
\]

Hence
\[
\cos lqsntbva=\frac{2 vlgwzhrp^{2}+xhjfqmle^{2}}{3 xhjfqmle vlgwzhrp}=\frac{2}{3} \frac{vlgwzhrp}{xhjfqmle}+\frac{1}{3} \frac{xhjfqmle}{vlgwzhrp} .
\]

Thus
\[
\angle yknrslbt oepmavcz wjsrilkq=\arccos \left(\frac{2}{3} \frac{vlgwzhrp}{xhjfqmle}+\frac{1}{3} \frac{xhjfqmle}{vlgwzhrp}\right)
\]

Since angles \( yknrslbt azdbqtnh oepmavcz \) and \( wjsrilkq yknrslbt oepmavcz \) are obtuse, we must have \( xhjfqmle<2 vlgwzhrp<2 xhjfqmle \). Since \( 2 trqkmdse / 3+1 / 3 trqkmdse<1 \) for \( \frac{1}{2}<trqkmdse<1 \), equation (1) determines a unique acute angle whenever \( 1 / 2<vlgwzhrp / xhjfqmle<1 \). Otherwise there is no equilibrium position except with \( oepmavcz \) on the wall.

Remark. If we calculate the height of the center of gravity of the rod, keeping \( yknrslbt \) directly below \( wjsrilkq \) on the wall, we find that the equilibrium position away from the wall is always unstable (i.e., a slight disturbance will tend to lower the center of gravity) while the vertical equilibrium is stable if and only if \( xhjfqmle<2 vlgwzhrp \).

kernel_variant.question:
A right-angled vertical corner is formed by the two mutually perpendicular, perfectly smooth walls  

   \Pi _1 : x = 0  and  \Pi _2 : y = 0 ,

whose common vertical edge is the z-axis \Gamma  (x = y = 0).  

Inside the corner a uniform heavy beam AB of length 3 k  (k > 0) and weight P is placed so that  

* its upper end A rests on the wall \Pi _1  (x_A = 0),  
* its lower end B rests on the wall \Pi _2  (y_B = 0),  
* the beam is not vertical (i.e. it is not parallel to \Gamma ).

Write, a priori,

  A = (0, y, a)     (y \geq  0, a > 0),  
  B = (x, 0, z)     (x > 0,  z < a);             (*)

(the coordinate z may even be negative because no horizontal floor is present).  

A light, inextensible string BC of fixed length 4 b (b > 0) joins the point B to a fixed point  

   C \equiv  (0, 0, h)   (h > 0)

on the edge \Gamma .  No other supports act.  The only forces on the system are  

 * P (the weight) acting vertically downward at the mid-point G of AB,  
 * T (the tension of the string) acting on B along the line CB,  
 * R_1 (the smooth reaction of \Pi _1) acting at A along the +e_x-direction,  
 * R_2 (the smooth reaction of \Pi _2) acting at B along the +e\gamma -direction.  

The system is in static equilibrium and the beam is not parallel to either wall.  
(N.B.  A line contained in a plane is not regarded as ``parallel'' to that plane.)

Introduce the positive parameters

 u := a - z  (the vertical rise of the beam),  d := h - a  (the extra rise of the string above A).   (**)

(i) Starting exclusively from the six equilibrium equations

  \Sigma F = 0,  \Sigma M_O = 0  (about the origin O),

prove that  

   y = 0  (so that A lies on the edge \Gamma ),

and that a non-vertical equilibrium is possible if and only if the two given lengths k, b satisfy the double inequality  

   2 b < 3 k < 4 b,            (1)

while the heights must obey the single relation  

   3 d^2 = 16 b^2 - 9 k^2  ( = 3 u^2 ).      (2)

Deduce that relation (2) fixes only the vertical separation d (= u) between A and C, whereas the absolute height a (and hence h = a + d) may be chosen arbitrarily large, giving a one-parameter family of equilibrium positions.

(ii) For any admissible triple (k, b, d) found in part (i) determine the acute angle  

   \varphi  = \angle ABC  

and show that  

   cos \varphi  = (9 k^2 + 8 b^2)/(18 k b).     (3)

(iii) Using (2)-(3) obtain explicit expressions for the tension T and for the wall reactions R_1, R_2, proving that  

   T  = (2 b P)/d = (2 b P \sqrt{3})/\sqrt{16 b^2 - 9 k^2},  
   R_1 = (P/2) \sqrt{ (36 k^2 - 16 b^2)/(16 b^2 - 9 k^2) } > 0,  
   R_2 \equiv  0.  

Discuss the behaviour of T and R_1 as k approaches the lower and the upper bounds in (1), and decide whether R_2 can ever be positive or negative.  Comment explicitly on the effect of a rigid vertical translation of the whole configuration.

---------------------------------------------------------------
kernel_variant.solution:
We use throughout the orthonormal basis  

 e_x = (1,0,0), e\gamma  = (0,1,0), e_z = (0,0,1).

--------------------------------------------------------------------
0. Notation and purely geometric relations
--------------------------------------------------------------------
With the points written as in (*) and the parameters defined in (**)

 u := a - z > 0,  d := h - a > 0,          (4)

the fixed lengths of the beam and of the string yield

 |AB|^2 = x^2 + y^2 + u^2 = (3 k)^2 = 9 k^2,       (5a)  
 |BC|^2 = x^2 + (u + d)^2     = (4 b)^2 = 16 b^2.    (5b)

No assumption about y has been made so far.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
1. Force equilibrium \Sigma F = 0
--------------------------------------------------------------------
The forces at A and B are  

 at A: R_1 e_x,  

 at B: R_2 e\gamma  + T (-x e_x + (u + d) e_z)/(4 b) - P e_z.

Hence

 \Sigma F_x : R_1 - T x/(4 b) = 0,            (6a)  
 \Sigma F_y : R_2 = 0,                 (6b)  
 \Sigma F_z : T(u + d)/(4 b) - P = 0.        (6c)

--------------------------------------------------------------------
2. Moment equilibrium \Sigma M_O = 0
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Position vectors

 r_A = (0, y, a), r_B = (x, 0, z), r_G = (x/2, y/2, (a + z)/2).

(i) Moment of R_1 : r_A \times  (R_1 e_x) = (0, a R_1, -y R_1).  
(ii) Moment of R_2 : r_B \times  (R_2 e\gamma ) = (-z R_2, 0, x R_2).  
(iii) Moment of T : CB = C - B = (-x, 0, u + d); so  

 r_B \times  [T CB/(4 b)] = (0, -T x(a + d)/(4 b), 0).  
(iv) Moment of P : r_G \times  (-P e_z) = (-P y/2, P x/2, 0).

Adding all four contributions and setting the result to zero yields  

 \Sigma M_x : -P y/2 - z R_2 = 0,           (7a)  
 \Sigma M_y : a R_1 - T x(a + d)/(4 b) + P x/2 = 0, (7b)  
 \Sigma M_z : -y R_1 + x R_2 = 0.           (7c)

--------------------------------------------------------------------
3. Point A lies on the edge \Gamma 
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Equation (6b) already gives R_2 = 0.  Substituting this into (7a) and (7c) gives  

 -P y/2 = 0 and -y R_1 = 0.

Because P > 0, we must have y = 0.  Therefore  

 A = (0, 0, a) \in  \Gamma .               (8)

Equation (7b) now simplifies to  

 a R_1 - T x(a + d)/(4 b) + P x/2 = 0.     (9)

--------------------------------------------------------------------
4. Compatibility of the six equilibrium equations
--------------------------------------------------------------------
From (6c)  T = 4 b P /(u + d).          (10)

Insert R_1 from (6a) into (9) and use (10).  Because x > 0 by hypothesis, we can divide by x:  

 a\cdot T/(4 b) - T(a + d)/(4 b) + P/2 = 0  
  \Leftrightarrow  T(-d)/(4 b) + P/2 = 0.

With T > 0 this yields  

 T = 2 b P/d.                (11)

Equating (10) and (11) gives  

 2 b P/d = 4 b P/(u + d) \Rightarrow  u = d.     (12)

Since y = 0, eqn (5a) becomes

 x^2 + u^2 = 9 k^2.               (13)

Eliminating x^2 between (13) and (5b) with u = d gives

 d^2 = (16 b^2 - 9 k^2)/3,   d > 0.     (14)

Positivity of d supplies 3 k < 4 b, while x^2 > 0 from (13) gives d < 3 k, i.e. 3 k > 2 b.  Altogether

 2 b < 3 k < 4 b,             (15)

and, rewriting (14),

 3 d^2 = 16 b^2 - 9 k^2  (= 3 u^2).     (16)

Thus (15) and (16) are the necessary and sufficient conditions for a non-vertical equilibrium.  Equation (16) fixes only the separation d ( = u ); the absolute height a is arbitrary, so the whole configuration can be translated vertically, providing a one-parameter family of equilibria.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
5. Angle between beam and string
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Because u = d,

 BA = A - B = (-x, 0, d),  BC = C - B = (-x, 0, 2 d).

Hence

 BA\cdot BC = x^2 + 2 d^2,  |BA| = 3 k, |BC| = 4 b,

so

 cos \varphi  = (x^2 + 2 d^2)/(12 k b).        (17)

Substituting x^2 = 9 k^2 - d^2 and d^2 from (16) gives

 cos \varphi  = (9 k^2 + 8 b^2)/(18 k b),      (18)

which is formula (3).

--------------------------------------------------------------------
6. Magnitudes of the unknown forces
--------------------------------------------------------------------
The two expressions (10) and (11) immediately yield

 T = (2 b P)/d = (2 b P \sqrt{3})/\sqrt{16 b^2 - 9 k^2}. (19)

Using (6a) together with x^2 = 9 k^2 - d^2,

 R_1 = T x/(4 b)  
   = P x/(2 d)  
   = (P/2) \sqrt{ (36 k^2 - 16 b^2)/(16 b^2 - 9 k^2) } > 0. (20)

Equation (6b) has already given  

 R_2 \equiv  0.                 (21)

--------------------------------------------------------------------
7. Limiting behaviour and vertical translation invariance
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Introduce r := 3 k/(4 b); the admissible range is \frac{1}{2} < r < 1.

(i) As r \downarrow  \frac{1}{2} (i.e. 3 k \to  2 b)

 d^2 \to  4 b^2, d \to  2 b, x^2 \to  0,  
 R_1 \to  0, T \to  P.

(ii) As r \uparrow  1 (i.e. 3 k \to  4 b)

 d \to  0^+, T \approx  2 b P/d \to  +\infty ,  
 R_1 \approx  (P/2)(2 x/d) \to  +\infty .

Throughout the admissible interval R_1 is strictly positive, while R_2 is identically zero; hence the wall \Pi _2 never exerts a positive or negative reaction---the contact at B is sustained purely by the string.

Finally, replacing every position vector r by r' = r + \tau  e_z (\tau  real) leaves each force unchanged and adds the same cancelling term \tau  e_z \times  ( * ) to every moment about O; therefore the equilibrium is invariant under any rigid vertical translation.  This corroborates that only the separation d ( = u ) is fixed.

--------------------------------------------------------------------

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
Compared with the original planar “rod-and-string-against-one-wall” problem, the present variant is appreciably harder because

1.  Three–dimensional setting  
 The rod touches two perpendicular walls; all unknowns (coordinates of B, length constraints, angles) live in R³ rather than R².

2.  Four forces instead of three  
 Alongside weight and string tension there are two distinct wall reactions.  
 One must therefore invoke concurrency of non-parallel forces and justify why one reaction vanishes.

3.  Non-linear elimination  
 The simultaneous equations (5)–(6)–(7) entail a quadratic substitution followed by an explicit non–negativity requirement, giving rise to the sharp double inequality (★).

4.  Messy algebra in angle computation  
 Finding cos φ requires inserting derived expressions for x and z and carefully manipulating quartic terms; a naïve approach is error-prone.

5.  Additional force analysis  
 Part (iii) demands resolution of forces in three orthogonal directions and discussion of the sign of R₂, something absent from the original problem.

Taken together these features oblige the solver to blend 3-D geometry, statics (concurrency, force resolution), and substantial algebraic manipulation—well beyond the scope of simple pattern matching or of the original two-dimensional equilibrium exercise.

original_kernel_variant.question:
A right-angled vertical corner is formed by the two mutually perpendicular, perfectly smooth walls  

   \Pi _1 : x = 0  and  \Pi _2 : y = 0 ,

whose common vertical edge is the z-axis \Gamma  (x = y = 0).  

Inside the corner a uniform heavy beam AB of length 3 k  (k > 0) and weight P is placed so that  

* its upper end A rests on the wall \Pi _1  (x_A = 0),  
* its lower end B rests on the wall \Pi _2  (y_B = 0),  
* the beam is not vertical (i.e. it is not parallel to \Gamma ).

Write, a priori,

  A = (0, y, a)     (y \geq  0, a > 0),  
  B = (x, 0, z)     (x > 0,  z < a);             (*)

(the coordinate z may even be negative because no horizontal floor is present).  

A light, inextensible string BC of fixed length 4 b (b > 0) joins the point B to a fixed point  

   C \equiv  (0, 0, h)   (h > 0)

on the edge \Gamma .  No other supports act.  The only forces on the system are  

 * P (the weight) acting vertically downward at the mid-point G of AB,  
 * T (the tension of the string) acting on B along the line CB,  
 * R_1 (the smooth reaction of \Pi _1) acting at A along the +e_x-direction,  
 * R_2 (the smooth reaction of \Pi _2) acting at B along the +e\gamma -direction.  

The system is in static equilibrium and the beam is not parallel to either wall.  
(N.B.  A line contained in a plane is not regarded as ``parallel'' to that plane.)

Introduce the positive parameters

 u := a - z  (the vertical rise of the beam),  d := h - a  (the extra rise of the string above A).   (**)

(i) Starting exclusively from the six equilibrium equations

  \Sigma F = 0,  \Sigma M_O = 0  (about the origin O),

prove that  

   y = 0  (so that A lies on the edge \Gamma ),

and that a non-vertical equilibrium is possible if and only if the two given lengths k, b satisfy the double inequality  

   2 b < 3 k < 4 b,            (1)

while the heights must obey the single relation  

   3 d^2 = 16 b^2 - 9 k^2  ( = 3 u^2 ).      (2)

Deduce that relation (2) fixes only the vertical separation d (= u) between A and C, whereas the absolute height a (and hence h = a + d) may be chosen arbitrarily large, giving a one-parameter family of equilibrium positions.

(ii) For any admissible triple (k, b, d) found in part (i) determine the acute angle  

   \varphi  = \angle ABC  

and show that  

   cos \varphi  = (9 k^2 + 8 b^2)/(18 k b).     (3)

(iii) Using (2)-(3) obtain explicit expressions for the tension T and for the wall reactions R_1, R_2, proving that  

   T  = (2 b P)/d = (2 b P \sqrt{3})/\sqrt{16 b^2 - 9 k^2},  
   R_1 = (P/2) \sqrt{ (36 k^2 - 16 b^2)/(16 b^2 - 9 k^2) } > 0,  
   R_2 \equiv  0.  

Discuss the behaviour of T and R_1 as k approaches the lower and the upper bounds in (1), and decide whether R_2 can ever be positive or negative.  Comment explicitly on the effect of a rigid vertical translation of the whole configuration.

---------------------------------------------------------------
original_kernel_variant.solution:
We use throughout the orthonormal basis  

 e_x = (1,0,0), e\gamma  = (0,1,0), e_z = (0,0,1).

--------------------------------------------------------------------
0. Notation and purely geometric relations
--------------------------------------------------------------------
With the points written as in (*) and the parameters defined in (**)

 u := a - z > 0,  d := h - a > 0,          (4)

the fixed lengths of the beam and of the string yield

 |AB|^2 = x^2 + y^2 + u^2 = (3 k)^2 = 9 k^2,       (5a)  
 |BC|^2 = x^2 + (u + d)^2     = (4 b)^2 = 16 b^2.    (5b)

No assumption about y has been made so far.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
1. Force equilibrium \Sigma F = 0
--------------------------------------------------------------------
The forces at A and B are  

 at A: R_1 e_x,  

 at B: R_2 e\gamma  + T (-x e_x + (u + d) e_z)/(4 b) - P e_z.

Hence

 \Sigma F_x : R_1 - T x/(4 b) = 0,            (6a)  
 \Sigma F_y : R_2 = 0,                 (6b)  
 \Sigma F_z : T(u + d)/(4 b) - P = 0.        (6c)

--------------------------------------------------------------------
2. Moment equilibrium \Sigma M_O = 0
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Position vectors

 r_A = (0, y, a), r_B = (x, 0, z), r_G = (x/2, y/2, (a + z)/2).

(i) Moment of R_1 : r_A \times  (R_1 e_x) = (0, a R_1, -y R_1).  
(ii) Moment of R_2 : r_B \times  (R_2 e\gamma ) = (-z R_2, 0, x R_2).  
(iii) Moment of T : CB = C - B = (-x, 0, u + d); so  

 r_B \times  [T CB/(4 b)] = (0, -T x(a + d)/(4 b), 0).  
(iv) Moment of P : r_G \times  (-P e_z) = (-P y/2, P x/2, 0).

Adding all four contributions and setting the result to zero yields  

 \Sigma M_x : -P y/2 - z R_2 = 0,           (7a)  
 \Sigma M_y : a R_1 - T x(a + d)/(4 b) + P x/2 = 0, (7b)  
 \Sigma M_z : -y R_1 + x R_2 = 0.           (7c)

--------------------------------------------------------------------
3. Point A lies on the edge \Gamma 
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Equation (6b) already gives R_2 = 0.  Substituting this into (7a) and (7c) gives  

 -P y/2 = 0 and -y R_1 = 0.

Because P > 0, we must have y = 0.  Therefore  

 A = (0, 0, a) \in  \Gamma .               (8)

Equation (7b) now simplifies to  

 a R_1 - T x(a + d)/(4 b) + P x/2 = 0.     (9)

--------------------------------------------------------------------
4. Compatibility of the six equilibrium equations
--------------------------------------------------------------------
From (6c)  T = 4 b P /(u + d).          (10)

Insert R_1 from (6a) into (9) and use (10).  Because x > 0 by hypothesis, we can divide by x:  

 a\cdot T/(4 b) - T(a + d)/(4 b) + P/2 = 0  
  \Leftrightarrow  T(-d)/(4 b) + P/2 = 0.

With T > 0 this yields  

 T = 2 b P/d.                (11)

Equating (10) and (11) gives  

 2 b P/d = 4 b P/(u + d) \Rightarrow  u = d.     (12)

Since y = 0, eqn (5a) becomes

 x^2 + u^2 = 9 k^2.               (13)

Eliminating x^2 between (13) and (5b) with u = d gives

 d^2 = (16 b^2 - 9 k^2)/3,   d > 0.     (14)

Positivity of d supplies 3 k < 4 b, while x^2 > 0 from (13) gives d < 3 k, i.e. 3 k > 2 b.  Altogether

 2 b < 3 k < 4 b,             (15)

and, rewriting (14),

 3 d^2 = 16 b^2 - 9 k^2  (= 3 u^2).     (16)

Thus (15) and (16) are the necessary and sufficient conditions for a non-vertical equilibrium.  Equation (16) fixes only the separation d ( = u ); the absolute height a is arbitrary, so the whole configuration can be translated vertically, providing a one-parameter family of equilibria.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
5. Angle between beam and string
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Because u = d,

 BA = A - B = (-x, 0, d),  BC = C - B = (-x, 0, 2 d).

Hence

 BA\cdot BC = x^2 + 2 d^2,  |BA| = 3 k, |BC| = 4 b,

so

 cos \varphi  = (x^2 + 2 d^2)/(12 k b).        (17)

Substituting x^2 = 9 k^2 - d^2 and d^2 from (16) gives

 cos \varphi  = (9 k^2 + 8 b^2)/(18 k b),      (18)

which is formula (3).

--------------------------------------------------------------------
6. Magnitudes of the unknown forces
--------------------------------------------------------------------
The two expressions (10) and (11) immediately yield

 T = (2 b P)/d = (2 b P \sqrt{3})/\sqrt{16 b^2 - 9 k^2}. (19)

Using (6a) together with x^2 = 9 k^2 - d^2,

 R_1 = T x/(4 b)  
   = P x/(2 d)  
   = (P/2) \sqrt{ (36 k^2 - 16 b^2)/(16 b^2 - 9 k^2) } > 0. (20)

Equation (6b) has already given  

 R_2 \equiv  0.                 (21)

--------------------------------------------------------------------
7. Limiting behaviour and vertical translation invariance
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Introduce r := 3 k/(4 b); the admissible range is \frac{1}{2} < r < 1.

(i) As r \downarrow  \frac{1}{2} (i.e. 3 k \to  2 b)

 d^2 \to  4 b^2, d \to  2 b, x^2 \to  0,  
 R_1 \to  0, T \to  P.

(ii) As r \uparrow  1 (i.e. 3 k \to  4 b)

 d \to  0^+, T \approx  2 b P/d \to  +\infty ,  
 R_1 \approx  (P/2)(2 x/d) \to  +\infty .

Throughout the admissible interval R_1 is strictly positive, while R_2 is identically zero; hence the wall \Pi _2 never exerts a positive or negative reaction---the contact at B is sustained purely by the string.

Finally, replacing every position vector r by r' = r + \tau  e_z (\tau  real) leaves each force unchanged and adds the same cancelling term \tau  e_z \times  ( * ) to every moment about O; therefore the equilibrium is invariant under any rigid vertical translation.  This corroborates that only the separation d ( = u ) is fixed.

--------------------------------------------------------------------

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
Compared with the original planar “rod-and-string-against-one-wall” problem, the present variant is appreciably harder because

1.  Three–dimensional setting  
 The rod touches two perpendicular walls; all unknowns (coordinates of B, length constraints, angles) live in R³ rather than R².

2.  Four forces instead of three  
 Alongside weight and string tension there are two distinct wall reactions.  
 One must therefore invoke concurrency of non-parallel forces and justify why one reaction vanishes.

3.  Non-linear elimination  
 The simultaneous equations (5)–(6)–(7) entail a quadratic substitution followed by an explicit non–negativity requirement, giving rise to the sharp double inequality (★).

4.  Messy algebra in angle computation  
 Finding cos φ requires inserting derived expressions for x and z and carefully manipulating quartic terms; a naïve approach is error-prone.

5.  Additional force analysis  
 Part (iii) demands resolution of forces in three orthogonal directions and discussion of the sign of R₂, something absent from the original problem.

Taken together these features oblige the solver to blend 3-D geometry, statics (concurrency, force resolution), and substantial algebraic manipulation—well beyond the scope of simple pattern matching or of the original two-dimensional equilibrium exercise.
